nativeRange.habitat: Shallow streams, pond margins, and seasonally flooded wetlands across tropical Central and South America; grows submerged, emersed, or floating on still and slow-moving water

decorPreferences: open midground or background space for trailing stems
open surface area if grown floating

goodWith: fry that benefit from floating cover
avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, large cichlids, or silver dollars

summary: Brazilian Pennywort (Hydrocotyle leucocephala) is a versatile, beginner-friendly stem plant with round, coin-shaped leaves on creeping stems. It can be planted, left to float at the surface, or trained along hardscape, and grows quickly under medium light.
careNotes: Brazilian Pennywort is a fast-growing stem plant native to Central and South America, recognizable by its round, coin-shaped leaves arranged singly along a long creeping stem. It is one of the more versatile aquarium plants available to beginners: it can be planted in substrate as a midground or background accent, left to float at the surface where it forms shade and cover for fry, or trained to trail along driftwood and rockwork. It is worth distinguishing from two related species — Hydrocotyle verticillata ([fw-plant-031]), which has round leaves arranged in whorls and grows as a strict low creeper, and Hydrocotyle sibthorpioides ([fw-plant-071]), which is a much smaller carpeting species. Care is generally beginner-level: Brazilian Pennywort tolerates a wide range of conditions (temperature roughly 20–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, soft to moderately hard water) and does not require CO2 injection, though added CO2, stronger light, and a nutrient-rich substrate tend to produce denser, more compact growth. Iron supplementation is commonly advised if the leaf edges begin to yellow. The plant can reach 13–25 cm in height when rooted, but trailing stems left untrimmed can grow considerably longer. Regular trimming encourages bushier growth and prevents the plant from shading out lower-growing species.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via vegetative cuttings: cut the creeping stem into segments and replant, or separate lateral shoots/daughter plants from a healthy mother plant. The species throws many lateral shoots along the stem that can be trimmed off anywhere and replanted, and frequent trimming encourages bushier, more compact growth.

primary.notes: Used for taxonomy (Araliaceae / Apiales), temperature (5-30 °C, optimum 20-28 °C), pH (6-7.5), kH (2-15), CO2 (10-40 mg/l), lighting (medium to high), growth rate (fast), care difficulty (very easy), and propagation.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (6.5-7.5), lighting (medium), CO2 (required), growth rate (moderate to fast), placement, propagation, and adult size (5-10 inches). Lists family as 'Apiaceae' (older taxonomy) and origin as 'Asia' — both rejected in favor of Flowgrow's modern taxonomy and the species' well-known Neotropical origin.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (59-82 °F = ~15-28 °C), lighting (medium to high), CO2 (not required, beneficial), growth rate (fast), placement versatility (midground, foreground, floating, trailing on hardscape), care level (easy), and leaf size (1-2 inch coin-shaped leaves).
